title: Household coordination can aid solar adoption - Nature Energy
text:
Coordination and active deliberation that includes the whole household can facilitate solar adoption. Interventions targeting energy adoption are likely to be more effective when they are designed to support collective decision making rather than focusing on a single member of the household.Messages for policy

Marketing and communication efforts promoting residential solar adoption can be designed to encourage constructive dialogue and facilitate coordination among family members.

Workshops can be designed to include facilitated discussions and/or structured discussion guides to help household members collectively navigate logistics, financial decisions, and information gathering.

Interventions can be timed to leverage opportune moments when households are in sync and external catalysts (such as escalating bills) trigger action.

Solar champions (that is, individuals who take primary responsibility for and bear much of the burden of the adoption process) can be supported by providing resources that reduce the burden of solar adoption, such as co-decision platforms or community workshops that foster collaboration.

You have full access to this article via your institution.

Download PDF

based on Sintov, N. D., Dillman-Hasso, N. H. & Hurst, K. F. Nat. Energy https://doi.org/10.1038/s41560-026-02104-8 (2026).The policy problemHuman behaviour plays a crucial role in meeting international climate targets. For example, home electrification technologies, such as residential rooftop solar, offer critical pathways to decarbonization, but only if households choose to adopt them. Prior work on the adoption of home decarbonization technologies tends to focus on household-level outcomes (for example, adoption versus non-adoption) and/or the views of a single household member. This overlooks the social complexity of household decision making, which is likely to occur through many conversations among household members over time and hinges on households’ capacities for coordination. Uncovering what underlies successful coordination can provide a deeper understanding of household decision-making processes and guide the design of solar solutions that are more likely to be adopted.The findingsWe examined how intrahousehold dynamics shape solar adoption by carrying out two studies. Study 1 identified two main patterns of household decision making. In sync couples shared a commitment to solar adoption and joint contribution to adoption logistics; this pattern was observed among adopters. Oppositional households were misaligned on solar goals, with one partner often acting as a ‘holdout’; these couples had either decided against adopting solar or were undecided. External catalysts such as expiring tax credits or escalating bills often propelled in sync households to adoption. Study 2 findings aligned with these patterns: members of adopter households, in particular parents and romantic partners, were more involved in the process, completed more solar-related tasks, and started more solar-related discussions than their counterparts in households that had not adopted solar. In both studies, ‘solar champions’ emerged from the adoption process. These findings apply to contexts wherein residential solar adoption involves multiple decision-makers, and may generalize to additional residential energy efficiency improvements.The studyAcross two studies, we investigated the dynamics of household decision making processes in the context of solar adoption using data collected in the USA. In Study 1, semi-structured qualitative interviews with 39 couples focused on interactions related to the decision to adopt rooftop solar (or not), as well as barriers and motivators to adoption. Seven couples (18%) had already adopted solar, seven (18%) had considered solar but ultimately decided not to adopt and 25 (64%) were still deciding. In Study 2, we surveyed N = 268 ‘lost customers’ who had considered solar but did not adopt and N = 126 who had adopted rooftop solar. Survey participants listed members of their household (including themselves) and indicated how each member was involved in the solar adoption decision-making process (for example, initiated discussions, executed tasks to advance adoption, level of support).

Further readingWolske, K. S., Gillingham, K. T. & Schultz, P. W. Peer influence on household energy behaviours. Nat. Energy 5, 202–212 (2020). This review examines prior work on social influence in the context of energy behaviour, particularly understanding when and how peers shape energy related behaviour.Article
